author: @justtinkering6713
text: What is needed is to replace the circular motion pedal crank with an up/down motion. When the pedals are moving horizontal there is no torque applied to the rear wheel. Lost motion. I'm thinking video 1980 1982 Luke's pump generator/flywheel.

author: @billunderwood5453
text: I read somewhere that Starley was working in his uncle's penny-farthing factory where the workers lifted the various parts down from a conveyor chain moving over their heads, and that chain was the inspiration for the pedal/chain/gear arrangement.

author: @spydocs
text: Lovely story of bicycling! I have a dream of building wind turbines and gearing them to old motor-turned-generators with recycled bicycle gears and chains. Your videos will be the source of a lot of my research. Best wishes

author: @davidtraugot1405
text: In Boston MA there is a nonprofit "Bikes Not Bombs" which collects donated and rescued bikes and ships them to project partners in the global south, one of which is a Central American group that makes agricultural machines, water pumps, whatever, with pedal power and sometimes cement-filled flywheels.  They train all their partners in mechanical and business basics, and aim to empower people with transportation - to school, to work, with products to markets, to bring about social change.

author: @lasentinal
text:
Recumbents are much faster than upright bicycles, but they were banned by the Union Cycliste Internationale (UCI) in 1934. Thus bicycle development was stifled by this irrational reaction.
I own a recumbent tadpole configured tricycle and I can ride for hours without getting tired. It has 63 gears, which makes going up steep inclines easy, although slow. I could motorize it, but there is no need to. At 71 years of age, this is my main mode of personal transport. You can apply up to 3 times your own body weight in power to the pedals because you are on a seat and not a testicle damaging saddle and when you stop for traffic lights, you don't have to take your feet off the peddles.

author: @neilbain8736
text: The Penny Farthing had another problem. The fit was governed by your inside leg length because you effectively had to sit on top of the wheel to reach the pedals and that dictated the size of the wheel. Short legs could never reach the pedals on a large wheel. The diameter of the wheel was also effectively the gear. Small wheels for short legs took less effort to turn than large wheels for long legs.

Im yet decide how to satisfy my energy needs. Currently Ive been enriching the gas companies beyond my wildest nightmares, keeping my generator running for juice. My elegoo neptune 4 arrives tomorrow and I cant wait to print your wind turbine

author: @mikeyb7263
text: I had a neighborhood friend many years ago whose father was an avid bicyclist. In fact he was part of the US Olympic team in 1948, 1952, and 1964.  His name was Thomas (Frank) Montemage. He had a homemade setup in his basement that had the rear wheel driving a generator that provided power to a small black and white TV mounted in front of the handlebars. My daughter owns a Peloton now. I told her she should say "thanks Frank" every time she starts a ride.

author: @bill_r
text: I fully agree that bicycles are terrific. I used to collect discarded bicycles, refurbish them and give them away.  Nowadays, capitalism and the greed of manufacturers means it is impossible to find discarded bicycles of the same wheel size 700mm, 27.5 in, 26 in x 1.5 wheel is different from 26 in x 1 1/2 wheel and so on.

author: @salimufari
text: I get a feeling that Robert is about to replace a flywheel tire of a stationary spinner with a magnet riddled rim rotor. Then replace the tire with a stationary serpentine coil. Need a stationary pedal generator anyone?

author: @grendel1960a
text: I got an electric bicycle, and now any trip under about 10 miles, I will use the bike, I do need to make a trailer for when I go shopping though as at the moment I still need to use the car for that (mind you with them shutting the local lidl, I am having to trek ever to Herne bay Lidl for shopping, so use the car for that.), but from someone who did 33,000 miles a year in the car, I now have it down to a few thousand.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: and yes, there are already 3d printed parts going om the bike, a bottle holder, a camera mount, and where I bought a handlebar riser, It needed a 10mm spacer to take up the slack so the headstock bearing could be adjusted (if it fails I will remake it in aluminium)

I think a bit of correction has to be made about diamond shape. Unlike any other frame, diamond one is based on, basically triangles, the strongest engineering pieces. It's basically a rolling truss.
So yes, there is point to it, it's stronger with less material, reduces weight.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Funny thing...

I'm taking pieces of a used [and originally rather expensive] exercise machine, and parts off of a pair of bicycles to make a recumbent quadracycle, mainly for the purpose of exercise, but also just to get around with when the weather is fair and I do not need to use a truck for anything I'm shopping for.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
I did similar to make a 'tadpole' recumbent. Presently building another with tilt-turn steering. 
Great fun making and riding them, a bit slow up hills though as you can't get out of the saddle to use body-weight, hence I'm going to fit e-assist to the next one.
Good luck with your project.
